summary: Jeff Atwood explores OpenID as a potential solution to the 'login explosion' problem where users must remember dozens of username/password combinations across websites. He walks through the OpenID authentication flow, acknowledging both its benefits (amortized account creation costs, one-click subsequent logins, no password storage for developers) and significant drawbacks (phishing risks, privacy concerns, usability issues, adoption inequality). Despite these criticisms, Atwood argues that OpenID represents a 'worse is better' solution worth pursuing for low-stakes credentials, even if it's unsuitable for banking-grade security. He calls on developers to adopt OpenID rather than creating yet another login system.
key_points: OpenID allows users to authenticate across multiple websites using a single set of credentials from a trusted provider, potentially solving the problem of managing 50+ different logins
The quality of OpenID experience depends heavily on provider choice - good providers use SSL, anti-phishing measures, and attribute exchange to share profile data automatically

Major criticisms include phishing vulnerability, privacy concerns (providers see all your logins), centralized risk if compromised, and the chicken-and-egg adoption problem between providers and consumers
Using URLs as identifiers instead of email addresses creates usability friction that makes adoption harder for average users
For developers, OpenID offers a huge advantage: not having to store passwords, which is 'the last business you want to be in'
money_quotes: I have fifty online logins, and I can't remember any of them!
The quality of your OpenID experience is heavily influenced by the provider you choose.
As an application developer, you no longer have to store passwords! That's a huge advantage, because storing passwords is the last business you want to be in.
Normal busy people will choose the familiar route time and time again.
It is a bit odd to ask users to associate themselves with an arbitrary URL instead of an email address.
I am willing to accept these tradeoffs for a partial worse is better solution.
There's absolutely no way I'd put my banking credentials behind an OpenID.
If we can't make OpenID work, at least for run of the mill, low-value credentials that litter the web in increasing numbers – what hope do we have of ever fixing the login explosion problem?
key_insight: The login explosion problem is painful enough that imperfect solutions like OpenID deserve serious consideration and collective improvement, especially for the majority of low-stakes credentials that don't require banking-grade security.
